title: How to Fade Dark Spots & Acne Marks (PIH and PIE)
description: Brown spots or red marks left behind after breakouts? Here's how to fade post-acne hyperpigmentation (PIH) and redness (PIE) — the gentle actives that work, and why sunscreen is the real secret.
painQuote: Getting frustrated with PIH/PIE — the marks just won't fade.
intro: The breakout heals, but it leaves a mark — a brown spot (PIH, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation) or a flat red/pink one (PIE, post-inflammatory erythema). They can hang around for months, and the wrong products make them worse.
The good news: the right gentle routine genuinely fades them over time. The catch most people miss — without daily sunscreen, you're undoing all of it. Here's what actually works.

sections.h2: PIH vs PIE — which do you have?
body: Press the mark gently: if it briefly disappears or whitens, it's PIE (a vascular red mark). If it stays brown/tan, it's PIH (excess pigment). Most people have a mix.
Both fade — PIH responds to pigment-calming actives, PIE to barrier support and time. Neither responds to scrubbing, which only inflames things further.
Azelaic acid is the gentle multitasker here — it helps fade brown marks and calm redness, and it's tolerable even for sensitive skin. Niacinamide supports the barrier and helps even tone over time.
Introduce one at a time, every other night to start, and give it weeks — not days.

This is the part people skip and then wonder why nothing works: UV exposure deepens dark spots and slows fading dramatically. Daily broad-spectrum SPF is the single most effective 'spot treatment' you own.
Wear it every morning, rain or shine, and reapply if you're outdoors.
Marks fade on the order of 8–12 weeks, sometimes longer for deeper PIH. Avoid picking, harsh scrubs, and stacking too many actives — irritation creates new marks.
Want this built around your skin type and budget? Take the quiz for a full routine.

faq.q: How long do dark spots take to fade?
faq.a: Typically 8–12 weeks of consistent care for surface marks; deeper or older pigmentation can take several months. Daily sunscreen is what makes the difference between fading and stalling.

faq.q: Should I use vitamin C for dark spots?
faq.a: Vitamin C can help with brightness, but it can be unstable and irritating for some. Azelaic acid and niacinamide are gentler starting points; add vitamin C later if your skin tolerates actives well.

faq.q: Will exfoliating scrubs speed it up?
faq.a: No — physical scrubs usually inflame marks and can cause more pigmentation. Gentle leave-on actives plus sun protection are the reliable route.
